sec-lc1.heading: How does deposition shape our coastlines?

sec-lc2.heading: What coastal landforms does deposition create?

content.text: I can explain depositional processes and describe the coastal landforms they create.
when material such as sand, mud or rocks is dropped by the water that was carrying it
when water moves sand, mud and rocks from one place to another
when a wave rushes up the beach
when the water from a wave retreats back into the sea
material that is moved by water or air and settles in another place
happens at the coast when a wave loses energy and can no longer carry its load.  This often happens in sheltered parts of the coast, like bays.

image.altText: Photograph of a beach strewn with plastic bottles and other litter washed up on the sand.

content.text: that it is carrying, such as sand, mud or rocks.
It also drops any other debris it has picked up, such as plastics and litter, which pollute the natural environment.

image.altText: Photograph of a pebble beach with a rocky headland and waves breaking on the shore.

content.text: When sediment, like sand and shingle, is deposited at the coast, it can create landforms, like beaches.
One of the main ways that
take place at the coast is through a process called longshore drift.
and other debris at the coast.
happens when waves have lots of energy.
Longshore drift occurs when waves approach the coast                        at an angle because of the direction of the prevailing wind.
is then transported along the coastline by the waves.

content.text: The process repeats itself along the coast, in a zigzag pattern.

content.altText: Diagram showing longshore drift repeating in a zigzag along the coast, with swash, backwash and prevailing wind labelled.

layers.altText: Illustration of waves moving sediment along a beach in a zigzag pattern by swash and backwash.

is waves flowing back down the beach toward the sea.

Number the statements 1 - 5 to show how a spit forms, where 1 is the first stage.
Sediment is transported along a beach by longshore drift.
If the wind direction changes over time, a hook is formed at the end of the spit.
A long, narrow ridge of land projects out of the coastline, called a spit.
There is a change in direction of the coastline.
Transportation and deposition of sediment continues in the original direction and sediment builds up over time.
often form behind a spit. This is a very sheltered area, as waves can’t get past the spit so they have low energy.
Water movement slows down enough so that even fine material like mud is deposited
Over time, this builds up and salt-tolerant plants start to grow.
Sometimes a spit grows across a bay joining two headlands. This is called a
The bar cuts off the water behind from the open sea and creates a
is formed when a spit connects the mainland coast to an island.
Where do salt marshes often form?

diagram.altText: Map of a coastline showing depositional landforms to be labelled: spit, salt marsh, tombolo, bar and lagoon.

layers.altText: Map of a coastline with depositional landforms marked for labelling.

image.altText: Map of a coastline showing depositional landforms for the extended-writing task.

content.text: 2. The picture shows a spit and a salt marsh.
Complete the boxes with short sentences to describe how they were formed.
1 Waves deposit sediment at the coast and longshore drift transports it along the coast.
2 When the coast changes direction, transportation and deposition continues happening in the original direction.
3 Sediment, like sand or stones, builds up over time creating a long narrow ridge called a spit.
4 The spit curves around into a hook shape where there is a change in wind direction.
5 Salt marshes form behind spits where waves have low energy; only salt-tolerant plants can grow there.
Longshore drift is the lateral movement of
Longshore drift creates depositional landforms such as spits, bars and tombolos.
Spits are long, narrow ridges of sand or shingle which project from a coastline into the sea.
Salt marshes may form behind spits, as these are low energy zones.
Spits can change direction due to the prevailing wind, which makes them curve into a hook.
I can explain depositional processes and describe the coastal landforms they create.
when material such as sand, mud or rocks                             is dropped by the water that was carrying it
when water moves sand, mud and rocks from one place to another
when a wave rushes up the beach
when the water from a wave retreats back into the sea
material that is moved by water or air and settles in another place
